questions.question: To comply with industry regulations, you will need to capture logs of all changes made to IAM roles and identities. Logs must be kept for 3 years. How would you meet this requirement?
options: Use Cloud Audit Logs and export them to Cloud Storage. Create a retention policy and retention policy lock to prevent the logs from being deleted prior to them reaching 3 years of age. Define a lifecycle policy to delete the logs after three years.

Use Cloud Audit Logs and export them to Bigtable. Create a retention policy and retention policy lock to prevent the logs from being deleted prior to them reaching 3 years of age. Define a lifecycle policy to delete the logs after three years.
Use Cloud Audit Logs and keep the logs in Cloud Logging. Specify a three year retention policy in Cloud Logging that automatically deletes the logs after three years.
Use Cloud Audit Logs and keep the logs in Cloud Monitoring. Specify a three year retention policy in Cloud Logging that automatically deletes the logs after three years.
answer: Use Cloud Audit Logs and export them to Cloud Storage. Create a retention policy and retention policy lock to prevent the logs from being deleted prior to them reaching 3 years of age. Define a lifecycle policy to delete the logs after three years.
questions.explanation: Cloud Audit log captures changes to IAM entities and keeps logs for 30 days. To keep them longer, export them to Cloud Storage. Use a retention policy to define how long the logs should be kept and using a retention policy lock to prevent changes to the retention period. Cloud Logging does not keep logs beyond 30 days and does not support retention policies. Cloud Monitoring collects and displays metrics, it does not store logs. Bigtable is not a good storage option for logs, it is designed for low latency writes at high volumes and provides for key lookups and queries that require range scanning. See https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/audit

questions.question: The performance of an application that uses Bigtable is starting to degrade as volumes grow. You suspect your row key design may not be optimal. You want to review access patterns for a group of row keys. What tool would you use?
options: Cloud Monitoring
Cloud Logging
Key Visualizer
Cloud Key Trace
answer: Key Visualizer
questions.explanation: Key Visualizer is a Bigtable tool for analyzing Bigtable usage patterns. Cloud Monitoring can be used for performance monitoring but it is not specifically designed to diagnose row key design problems. Cloud Logging can help when reviewing events in Bigtable but it is not designed to support row key design problems. There is no Google Cloud tool called Cloud Key Trace. See https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/keyvis-overview

questions.question: You want to monitor a Cloud Dataflow job and know the maximum duration that an item has been waiting in the pipeline. What Cloud Monitoring metric would you use to track the maximum duration?
options: job/data_watermark_age
job/system_lag
job/elapsed_time
job/element_count
answer: job/system_lag
questions.explanation: The correct answer is job/system_lag. The job/data_watermark_age is the age of the most recent item that‘s been fully processed by the pipeline. Job/elapsed_time is the elapsed time of the pipeline run time. Job/element_count is the number of items processed in a PCollection for the Read_input and Process_element transforms. See https://cloud.google.com/architecture/building-production-ready-data-pipelines-using-dataflow-monitoring

questions.question: How can you resolve a quotaExceeded error when updating a CSV with 1 million records in BigQuery?
options: Limit the number of records updated each day to avoid hitting the BigQuery UPDATE DML statement limit.
Increase the BigQuery UPDATE DML statement limit in the Quota management section of the Google Cloud Platform Console
. Split the source CSV file into smaller CSV files in Cloud Storage to reduce the number of BigQuery UPDATE DML statements per BigQuery job.
Import the new records from the CSV file into a new BigQuery table. Merge the new records with the existing records using a BigQuery job and write the results to a new BigQuery table.
answer: Import the new records from the CSV file into a new BigQuery table. Merge the new records with the existing records using a BigQuery job and write the results to a new BigQuery table.
questions.explanation: Correct option D. Import the new records from the CSV file into a new BigQuery table. Merge the new records with the existing records using a BigQuery job and write the results to a new BigQuery table. Why D is Correct quotaExceeded on UPDATE DML occurs due to tabledata update limits (15K rows/day default). MERGE → new table bypasses this by using CTAS (CREATE TABLE AS SELECT) pattern: sql MERGE target t USING source s ON t.id = s.id WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE ... WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ... -- Results written to NEW table (no quota hit) Incorrect option A. Limit records updated each day Doesn’t resolve quotaExceeded – still hits DML row limit. option B. Increase UPDATE DML quota Not user-configurable – tabledata quotas cannot be increased. option C. Split CSV into smaller files Multiple UPDATEs = multiple quota violations. Still fails.

questions.question: Your Cloud Functions written in Node.js retrieve messages from Cloud Pub/Sub and forward the data to BigQuery. You notice that the message processing rate on the Pub/Sub topic is much higher than expected, and no errors are reported in Cloud Logging. Which two of the following are the most likely causes of this issue. (Choose two.)?
options: The publisher throughput quota is too small
The total number of outstanding messages exceeds the 10 MB limit
The subscriber code does not handle runtime errors properly
The subscriber code is unable to keep up with the message volume.
The subscriber code does not acknowledge the messages it pulls.
answer: The subscriber code is unable to keep up with the message volume.
The subscriber code does not acknowledge the messages it pulls.
questions.explanation: Correct Option D. The subscriber code is unable to keep up with the message volume. If the subscriber (Cloud Function) cannot process messages fast enough, Pub/Sub will continue delivering messages at a high rate. This mismatch between incoming message volume and subscriber processing capacity is a common cause of unexpected throughput behavior. Correct because it directly explains why the processing rate is higher than expected. Option E. The subscriber code does not acknowledge the messages it pulls. In Pub/Sub, messages must be acknowledged by the subscriber. If acknowledgments are missing, Pub/Sub will redeliver the same messages, inflating the observed processing rate. Correct because unacknowledged messages cause repeated delivery, which looks like higher throughput. Incorrect Option A. The publisher throughput quota is too small. If the publisher quota were too small, fewer messages would be published, not more. This would reduce throughput, not increase it. Incorrect because the issue is about unexpectedly high processing rate, not low publishing capacity. Option B. The total number of outstanding messages exceeds the 10 MB limit. Pub/Sub does not enforce a strict 10 MB outstanding message limit in this way. Message backlog issues would cause delays or throttling, not higher-than-expected throughput. Incorrect because this does not explain the observed behavior. Option C. The subscriber code does not handle runtime errors properly. If runtime errors were not handled, you would expect errors in Cloud Logging. Since the scenario states no errors are reported, this is not the likely cause.

questions.question: You have started a batch job in Dataflow, and it has processed a few elements before suddenly failing and shutting down. You investigate the Dataflow monitoring interface and find errors related to a specific DoFn in your pipeline. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these errors?
options: Validation of the job
Errors in the worker code
Construction of the pipeline or graph
Insufficient permissions
answer: Errors in the worker code
questions.explanation: The most likely cause of errors related to a particular DoFn in a failed Dataflow batch job after processing a few elements is B: Errors/Exceptions in worker code. This means that there is an issue with the code written in the DoFn that is causing it to throw an exception and fail. It could be due to incorrect input/output types, null pointer exceptions, or other coding errors. The error messages in the Dataflow monitoring interface should provide more information about the specific cause of the error, which can help in debugging the issue

questions.question: The marketing team at your organization provides regular updates of a segment of your customer dataset. The marketing team has given you a CSV with 1 million records that must be updated in BigQuery. When you use the UPDATE statement in BigQuery, you receive a quotaExceeded error. What should you do?
options: Reduce the number of records updated each day to stay within the BigQuery UPDATE DML statement limit.
Increase the BigQuery UPDATE DML statement limit in the Quota management section of the Google Cloud Platform Console.
Split the source CSV file into smaller CSV files in Cloud Storage to reduce the number of BigQuery UPDATE DML statements per BigQuery job.
Import the new records from the CSV file into a new BigQuery table. Create a BigQuery job that merges the new records with the existing records and writes the results to a new BigQuery table.
answer: Import the new records from the CSV file into a new BigQuery table. Create a BigQuery job that merges the new records with the existing records and writes the results to a new BigQuery table.

questions.question: You have Cloud Functions written in Node.js that pull messages from Cloud Pub/Sub and send the data to BigQuery. You observe that the message processing rate on the Pub/Sub topic is orders of magnitude higher than anticipated, but there is no error logged in Stackdriver Log Viewer. What are the two most likely causes of this problem? (Choose two.)
options: Publisher throughput quota is too small.
Total outstanding messages exceed the 10-MB maximum.
Error handling in the subscriber code is not handling run-time errors properly.
The subscriber code cannot keep up with the messages.
The subscriber code does not acknowledge the messages that it pulls.
answer: Error handling in the subscriber code is not handling run-time errors properly.
The subscriber code does not acknowledge the messages that it pulls.
questions.explanation: Answer: C, E Description: C, E: By not acknowleding the pulled message, this result in it be putted back in Cloud Pub/Sub, meaning the messages accumulate instead of being consumed and removed from Pub/Sub. The same thing can happen ig the subscriber maintains the lease on the message it receives in case of an error. This reduces the overall rate of processing because messages get stuck on the first subscriber. Also, errors in Cloud Function do not show up in Stackdriver Log Viewer if they are not correctly handled. A: No problem with publisher rate as the observed result is a higher number of messages and not a lower number. B: if messages exceed the 10MB maximum, they cannot be published. D: Cloud Functions automatically scales so they should be able to keep up.

questions.question: How can you enable each analytics team to monitor BigQuery slot usage within their respective projects?
options: Build a Cloud Monitoring dashboard using the BigQuery metric query/scanned_bytes.
Develop a Cloud Monitoring dashboard utilizing the BigQuery metric slots/allocated_for_project.
Export a log for each project, collect the BigQuery job execution logs, create a custom metric based on the totalSlotMs, and create a Cloud Monitoring dashboard based on the custom metric.

Export an aggregated log at the organization level, collect the BigQuery job execution logs, create a custom metric based on the totalSlotMs, and build a Cloud Monitoring dashboard based on the custom metric.
answer: Develop a Cloud Monitoring dashboard utilizing the BigQuery metric slots/allocated_for_project.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. Create a Cloud Monitoring dashboard based on the BigQuery metric slots/allocated_for_project. This option allows each team to monitor the number of slots allocated to their project and how many of them are currently in use, giving them visibility into their current and historical usage. Ref: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reservations-monitoring#viewing-slot-usage Option A is incorrect because it monitors data volume, not slot usage. Option C is inefficient because it requires creating a custom metric for each project and may result in a large number of metrics. Option D is also inefficient and may result in a large number of logs being exported, making it difficult to monitor slot usage for each project separately

questions.question: Scenario: As an orchestrator of ETL pipelines using Cloud Composer, a task in the Apache Airflow directed acyclic graph (DAG) depends on a third-party service. How can you ensure you are notified when the task fails?
options: Assign a function with notification logic to the on_retry_callback parameter for the operator responsible for the task at risk.
Configure a Cloud Monitoring alert on the sla_missed metric associated with the task at risk to trigger a notification.
Assign a function with notification logic to the on_failure_callback parameter tor the operator responsible for the task at risk.
Assign a function with notification logic to the sla_miss_callback parameter for the operator responsible for the task at risk.
answer: Assign a function with notification logic to the on_failure_callback parameter tor the operator responsible for the task at risk.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C. Assign a function with notification logic to the on_failure_callback parameter for the operator responsible for the task at risk.In Apache Airflow, the on_failure_callback parameter allows you to specify a function that will be called when a task fails. By assigning a function with notification logic to the on_failure_callback parameter for the operator responsible for the task at risk, you can be notified when the task does not succeed.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:A. Assign a function with notification logic to the on_retry_callback parameter for the operator responsible for the task at risk.This option is incorrect because the on_retry_callback parameter is triggered when a task is retried, not when it fails. It is not suitable for notifying you when the task does not succeed.B. Configure a Cloud Monitoring alert on the sla_missed metric associated with the task at risk to trigger a notification.This option is incorrect because configuring a Cloud Monitoring alert for SLA (Service Level Agreement) misses is useful for monitoring the performance of tasks based on their expected completion times. However, it does not directly address the need to be notified when a task fails.D. Assign a function with notification logic to the sla_miss_callback parameter for the operator responsible for the task at risk.This option is incorrect because there is no sla_miss_callback parameter in Apache Airflow. It is not a valid parameter for handling task failures and triggering notifications.

questions.question: Scenario: You are responsible for overseeing your company‘s data lake stored on BigQuery. The data ingestion pipelines are set up to retrieve data from Pub/Sub and store it in tables on BigQuery. Recently, there was a 50% increase in the daily stored data after a new version of the ingestion pipelines was implemented. Although the data volumes in Pub/Sub have not changed, some tables experienced a doubling in their daily partition data size. What steps should you take to investigate and resolve the reason behind the sudden increase in data?
options: 1. Check for duplicate rows in the BigQuery tables that have the daily partition data size doubled. 2. Schedule daily SQL jobs to deduplicate the affected tables. 3. Share the deduplication script with the other operational teams to reuse if this occurs to other tables.

1. Check for code errors in the deployed pipelines. 2. Check for multiple writing to pipeline BigQuery sink. 3. Check for errors in Cloud Logging during the day of the release of the new pipelines. 4. If no errors, restore the BigQuery tables to their content before the last release by using time travel.

1. Check for duplicate rows in the BigQuery tables that have the daily partition data size doubled. 2. Check the BigQuery Audit logs to find job IDs. 3. Use Cloud Monitoring to determine when the identified Dataflow jobs started and the pipeline code version. 4. When more than one pipeline ingests data into a table, stop all versions except the latest one.

1. Roll back the last deployment. 2. Restore the BigQuery tables to their content before the last release by using time travel. 3. Restart the Dataflow jobs and replay the messages by seeking the subscription to the timestamp of the release.
answer: 1. Check for duplicate rows in the BigQuery tables that have the daily partition data size doubled. 2. Check the BigQuery Audit logs to find job IDs. 3. Use Cloud Monitoring to determine when the identified Dataflow jobs started and the pipeline code version. 4. When more than one pipeline ingests data into a table, stop all versions except the latest one.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C.1. Check for duplicate rows in the BigQuery tables that have the daily partition data size doubled: This step is crucial as it helps identify if there are any duplicate rows causing the increase in data size.2. Check the BigQuery Audit logs to find job IDs: By checking the Audit logs, you can identify the specific jobs that were responsible for writing the data into the tables. This can help pinpoint the exact cause of the data increase.3. Use Cloud Monitoring to determine when the identified Dataflow jobs started and the pipeline code version: Cloud Monitoring can provide insights into when the Dataflow jobs started and the versions of the pipeline code used. This information can be useful in understanding the changes that led to the data increase.4. When more than one pipeline ingests data into a table, stop all versions except the latest one: Stopping older versions of the pipelines can help prevent further duplication of data and ensure that only the latest version is active.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:– Option A: This option focuses on deduplicating the affected tables but does not address the root cause of the data increase. It‘s important to first investigate and identify why the data size doubled before deduplicating.– Option B: This option suggests checking for code errors and errors in Cloud Logging, which are valid steps. However, the focus should be on investigating the cause of the data size increase rather than rolling back or restoring tables immediately.– Option D: Rolling back the deployment and restoring tables should be considered as a last resort after investigating the root cause of the data increase. It‘s important to understand and address the underlying issue before reverting changes.In conclusion, Option C provides a systematic approach to investigate the data increase by checking for duplicates, auditing job logs, monitoring job start times, and managing pipeline versions effectively.

questions.question: Scenario: Several data processing jobs were deployed into your Cloud Composer 2 environment. Some tasks are failing in Apache Airflow, and an increase in total workers memory usage with worker pod evictions is observed on the monitoring dashboard. What should you do to resolve these errors? (Choose two.)
options: Increase the directed acyclic graph (DAG) file parsing interval.
Increase the Cloud Composer 2 environment size from medium to large.
Increase the maximum number of workers and reduce worker concurrency.
Increase the memory available to the Airflow workers.
Increase the memory available to the Airflow triggerer.
answer: Increase the Cloud Composer 2 environment size from medium to large.
Increase the maximum number of workers and reduce worker concurrency.
questions.explanation: Increase the maximum number of workers and reduce worker concurrency Increase the Cloud Composer 2 environment size from medium to large Explanation: In your Cloud Composer 2 environment (which is based on Apache Airflow on GKE Autopilot), you are seeing: Task failures High memory usage by workers Worker pod evictions These symptoms suggest resource contention, especially memory pressure. 1. Increase the maximum number of workers and reduce worker concurrency More workers allow better distribution of tasks across pods. Reducing concurrency (tasks per worker) helps lower the chance of running out of memory per pod. 2. Increase the Cloud Composer 2 environment size Composer 2 has predefined sizes (e.g., medium, large, xlarge). Moving to a larger size increases GKE Autopilot resource quotas and allows the environment to scale more smoothly. Why the others are incorrect: “Increase the DAG file parsing interval”– This affects scheduling latency, not memory usage or task execution. “Increase memory to Airflow workers”– In Composer 2, you cannot directly control individual worker pod memory. It’s managed by GKE Autopilot. “Increase memory for the triggerer”– The triggerer handles deferred tasks (e.g., sensors). It’s not likely the cause of worker pod evictions.

questions.question: Scenario: As a maintainer of ETL pipelines, you observe a streaming pipeline in Dataflow that is experiencing delays in processing incoming data due to the automatic optimization and merging of the pipeline graph into one step. How can you determine the potential bottleneck in the streaming pipeline on Dataflow?
options: Insert a Reshuffle operation after each processing step, and monitor the execution details in the Dataflow console.
Insert output sinks after each key processing step, and observe the writing throughput of each block.
Log debug information in each ParDo function, and analyze the logs at execution time.
Verify that the Dataflow service accounts have appropriate permissions to write the processed data to the output sinks.
answer: Insert a Reshuffle operation after each processing step, and monitor the execution details in the Dataflow console.
questions.explanation: A. Insert a Reshuffle operation after each processing step, and monitor the execution details in the Dataflow console.– By inserting a Reshuffle operation after each processing step, you can ensure that the data is evenly distributed and shuffled properly between the steps. This can help in optimizing the data processing and potentially alleviate any bottlenecks caused by uneven data distribution.– Monitoring the execution details in the Dataflow console will provide insights into the performance of each step, allowing you to identify where the potential bottleneck is occurring.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Insert output sinks after each key processing step, and observe the writing throughput of each block.– This option focuses on observing the writing throughput of each block, which may not directly address the bottleneck issue in the processing speed. It is important to identify and address the bottleneck first before optimizing the writing throughput.C. Log debug information in each ParDo function, and analyze the logs at execution time.– Logging debug information in each ParDo function can be helpful for debugging purposes but may not directly help in identifying and addressing the bottleneck issue in the pipeline‘s processing speed.D. Verify that the Dataflow service accounts have appropriate permissions to write the processed data to the output sinks.– While ensuring appropriate permissions are important for data writing, this option does not address the issue of bottleneck in the processing speed of the pipeline. It is a necessary step but not directly related to identifying and resolving the bottleneck.

questions.question: Scenario: As a BigQuery admin supporting a team of data consumers using tools like Looker for ad hoc queries and reporting, you have observed slowness in query results. You suspect job queuing or slot contention as the cause, impacting performance. How can you investigate query job information to identify where the slowdowns are occurring and address the performance issues?
options: Use slot reservations for your project to ensure that you have enough query processing capacity and are able to allocate available slots to the slower queries.
Use Cloud Monitoring to view BigQuery metrics and set up alerts that let you know when a certain percentage of slots were used.
Use available administrative resource charts to determine how slots are being used and how jobs are performing over time. Run a query on the INFORMATION_SCHEMA to review query performance.
Use Cloud Logging to determine if any users or downstream consumers are changing or deleting access grants on tagged resources.
answer: Use available administrative resource charts to determine how slots are being used and how jobs are performing over time. Run a query on the INFORMATION_SCHEMA to review query performance.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C. Use available administrative resource charts to determine how slots are being used and how jobs are performing over time. Run a query on the INFORMATION_SCHEMA to review query performance.– Using available administrative resource charts will allow you to visually analyze how slots are being utilized and how jobs are performing over time. This can help you identify any patterns or bottlenecks that may be causing the slowdown in query results.– Running a query on the INFORMATION_SCHEMA will provide you with detailed information about query performance, such as execution times, bytes processed, and slot usage. This data can help you pinpoint specific queries or jobs that are causing performance issues.Why the other options are incorrect:A. Using slot reservations for your project may help ensure query processing capacity but does not directly address the need to investigate where performance issues are occurring. It focuses more on resource allocation rather than troubleshooting performance.B. While Cloud Monitoring can provide BigQuery metrics and alerts, it does not offer detailed query performance information that is needed to troubleshoot slowdowns in query results.D. Cloud Logging can be useful for tracking changes and access grants, but it does not provide the specific query performance data needed to troubleshoot performance issues related to job queuing or slot contention.

questions.question: Scenario: The marketing team at your organization regularly updates a segment of your customer dataset. They have provided you with a CSV containing 1 million records that need to be updated in BigQuery. What steps should you take when encountering a quotaExceeded error while using the UPDATE statement in BigQuery?
options: Reduce the number of records updated each day to stay within the BigQuery UPDATE DML statement limit.
Increase the BigQuery UPDATE DML statement limit in the Quota management section of the Google Cloud Platform Console.
Split the source CSV file into smaller CSV files in Cloud Storage to reduce the number of BigQuery UPDATE DML statements per BigQuery job.
Import the new records from the CSV file into a new BigQuery table. Create a BigQuery job that merges the new records with the existing records and writes the results to a new BigQuery table.
answer: Import the new records from the CSV file into a new BigQuery table. Create a BigQuery job that merges the new records with the existing records and writes the results to a new BigQuery table.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is option D: Import the new records from the CSV file into a new BigQuery table. Create a BigQuery job that merges the new records with the existing records and writes the results to a new BigQuery table.– The UPDATE statement in BigQuery is not supported for directly updating a large number of records in a table due to the quota limitations. It is more suitable for updating a smaller set of records.– By importing the new records into a new BigQuery table and merging them with the existing records, you can avoid the quotaExceeded error associated with the UPDATE statement.– This approach allows you to perform the update operation efficiently without hitting the quota limits of the UPDATE statement.Why the other options are incorrect:A. Reduce the number of records updated each day to stay within the BigQuery UPDATE DML statement limit.– This option suggests reducing the number of records updated each day, but it may not be a practical solution if you have a large dataset with 1 million records. It does not address the quotaExceeded error directly.B. Increase the BigQuery UPDATE DML statement limit in the Quota management section of the Google Cloud Platform Console.– The quota limits for BigQuery UPDATE statements are set by Google Cloud Platform and cannot be directly increased by users. Trying to increase the limit through the console will not resolve the quotaExceeded error.C. Split the source CSV file into smaller CSV files in Cloud Storage to reduce the number of BigQuery UPDATE DML statements per BigQuery job.– While splitting the CSV file could reduce the number of UPDATE statements per job, it does not address the fundamental issue of hitting the quota limit for the UPDATE statement. This approach may introduce unnecessary complexity and overhead.In conclusion, option D is the most appropriate solution for updating a large dataset in BigQuery without encountering quotaExceeded errors related to the UPDATE statement.

questions.question: Scenario: Each analytics team in your organization is executing BigQuery jobs in their respective projects. You aim to empower each team to track slot usage within their projects. What steps should be taken to achieve this goal?
options: Create a Cloud Monitoring dashboard based on the BigQuery metric query/scanned_bytes
Create a Cloud Monitoring dashboard based on the BigQuery metric slots/allocated_for_project
Create a log export for each project, capture the BigQuery job execution logs, create a custom metric based on the totalSlotMs, and create a Cloud Monitoring dashboard based on the custom metric

Create an aggregated log export at the organization level, capture the BigQuery job execution logs, create a custom metric based on the totalSlotMs, and create a Cloud Monitoring dashboard based on the custom metric
answer: Create a Cloud Monitoring dashboard based on the BigQuery metric slots/allocated_for_project
questions.explanation: Correct Option B. Create a Cloud Monitoring dashboard based on the BigQuery metric slots/allocated_for_project Correct. This metric directly tracks slot usage at the project level. By creating a Cloud Monitoring dashboard using this metric, each team can monitor how many slots are allocated to their jobs, which is exactly the requirement. Incorrect Option A. Create a Cloud Monitoring dashboard based on the BigQuery metric query/scanned_bytes Incorrect. This metric measures the number of bytes scanned by queries, which relates to query cost and performance, not slot usage. Option C. Create a log export for each project, capture the BigQuery job execution logs, create a custom metric based on the totalSlotMs, and create a Cloud Monitoring dashboard based on the custom metric Incorrect. While technically possible, this is unnecessarily complex. Slot usage can be tracked directly using built‑in metrics (slots/allocated_for_project), so creating custom metrics is not required. Option D. Create an aggregated log export at the organization level, capture the BigQuery job execution logs, create a custom metric based on the totalSlotMs, and create a Cloud Monitoring dashboard based on the custom metric Incorrect. This approach aggregates logs across the organization, which does not meet the requirement of empowering each team to track slot usage within their own projects. It also adds unnecessary overhead compared to using the built‑in metric.

questions.question: Scenario: After issuing a new batch job to Dataflow, the job successfully starts, processes a few elements, but suddenly fails and shuts down. Errors related to a specific DoFn in the pipeline are found in the Dataflow monitoring interface. What is the probable cause of these errors?
options: Job validation
Exceptions in worker code
Graph or pipeline construction
Insufficient permissions
answer: Exceptions in worker code
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. Exceptions in worker code.When the Dataflow job starts successfully, processes a few elements, and then suddenly fails and shuts down with errors related to a particular DoFn in your pipeline, it is most likely due to exceptions occurring in the worker code. A DoFn (Dataflow Function) is a user-defined function that processes elements in a Dataflow pipeline. If there are bugs, logic errors, or exceptions in the worker code within the DoFn, it can lead to failures during job execution.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:A. Job validation: Job validation typically occurs before the job starts running and checks for issues like missing or incorrect parameters, dependencies, or configurations. If the job passed the validation stage and started successfully but later failed during execution, job validation is not the most likely cause of the errors.C. Graph or pipeline construction: Graph or pipeline construction involves defining the data processing steps and dependencies in a Dataflow pipeline. While errors in graph or pipeline construction can lead to job failures, the scenario described in the question where the job starts successfully and then fails due to errors in a specific DoFn indicates that the issue lies within the worker code rather than the overall pipeline construction.D. Insufficient permissions: Insufficient permissions would prevent the Dataflow job from starting or accessing required resources, but it would not cause the specific errors related to a particular DoFn during job execution as described in the question. If permissions were the issue, the job would likely not have started successfully in the first place.In conclusion, when errors occur related to a specific DoFn during Dataflow job execution, the most likely cause is exceptions in the worker code within that particular function.

questions.question: A developer is creating a dashboard to monitor a service that uses Cloud Pub/Sub. They want to know when applications that read data from a pull subscription in Cloud Pub/Sub are not keeping up with the messages being ingested. What metric would you recommend they monitor?
options: subscription/excess_ingestion_volume
topic/excess_ingestion_volume
topic/num_undelivered_messages
subscription/num_undelivered_messages
answer: subscription/num_undelivered_messages
questions.explanation: The subscription/num_undelivered_messages is the count of undelivered messages an one metric to indicate how well subscribers are keeping up with ingestion. The metric is tracked for subscriptions not topics. There is no metric called excess_ingestion_rate. See https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/monitoring
